rationale: Severity pins are real, not decorative. I mutation-tested this empirically: in a scratch copy of 62cd6df3 (not the shared worktree), flipping both new r.AddError calls (impacted-domains-resolve, adr-touchpoint-missing) to r.AddWarning makes exactly the three failing-case subtests fail (path-like-zero-owner-fails, bare-id-anchor-missing-fails, filename-form-anchor-missing-fails) via both the direct SevError assertion and the redundant HasFailures() assertion, while the passing-case subtests stay green. An AddWarning-typed implementation would not survive this test file. Issue-code hygiene is clean: impacted-domains-resolve is reused byte-identical to plan.go/divergence.go (same code, same AddError call shape); adr-touchpoint-missing is a new, non-colliding code following the existing adr-cite-missing/adr-coverage-missing kebab-case convention. Regex probing (`\[(ADR-\d{4})[^\]]*\]\([^)]+\)`) against nested brackets, empty tails, and embedded newlines found two low-severity, non-blocking gaps (below), neither tested nor exercised by any current repo spec, and neither weakens the severity/typing contract this lens is scoped to.
findings: Non-blocking: a touchpoint link whose text contains a nested `]` before the real closing bracket (e.g. `[ADR-0031 [see note]](url)`) fails to match adrTouchpointLinkRe at all (verified: FindAllStringSubmatch returns zero matches), so a dangling ADR reference written in that shape silently passes with no adr-touchpoint-missing — a false negative via total omission rather than a wrong severity. Requires an author to hand-write brackets inside the touchpoint line itself; no ADR filename in the repo does this today.

Informational/forward-compat: the ID capture is `ADR-\d{4}` (exactly 4 digits), but internal/adr/parse.go NextID/NextIDAcross format new IDs with unclamped `%04d`, so once the store passes ADR-9999 a 5-digit ID like ADR-12345 would truncate under this regex to the wrong ID 'ADR-1234' (verified empirically). Zero practical risk at the repo's current ~40 ADRs; flagging only because no other code path in the repo previously encoded a 4-digit ADR-ID assumption via regex to compare against (adr_citations resolution reads structured YAML IDs, not regex-scraped prose), so this is a new parsing surface rather than an inherited convention.

Confirmed correct (not a finding, ruling out a suspected edge case): embedded newlines inside the link text or href tail still match correctly, since `[^\]]*`/`[^)]+` are negated character classes (not `.`) and cross newlines in Go's RE2 — no cross-line greedy-overconsumption bug into an adjacent touchpoint bullet.
